client.name: Netflix
client.businessModel: Subscription streaming for movies and TV shows, plus original productions

glossary.definition: Subscription Video On Demand — the streaming model Netflix pioneered.
glossary.whyItMatters: Defines how Netflix measures success: subscriber growth, engagement, and retention rather than ad revenue.

glossary.definition: The set of titles currently available to stream on Netflix in a given region.
glossary.whyItMatters: The dataset is a snapshot of the catalog at a point in time. Titles drop off when licensing expires.

glossary.definition: Titles produced or commissioned by Netflix, not licensed from a studio.
glossary.whyItMatters: Originals are Netflix's differentiator and biggest spend lever. The 'what to make' question is mostly about originals.

glossary.definition: Age-appropriateness rating: TV-Y, TV-Y7, TV-G, TV-PG, TV-14, TV-MA for TV shows; G, PG, PG-13, R for films.
glossary.whyItMatters: Rating maps to which audiences a title can reach. TV-MA limits the addressable market.

glossary.definition: The dataset's genre field. A comma-separated list — a single title can be tagged with multiple genres.
glossary.whyItMatters: Genres are not exclusive. A documentary about food is both 'Documentaries' and 'International Movies' depending on origin.

glossary.definition: The date a title was added to the Netflix catalog. Different from release_year.
glossary.whyItMatters: Reflects Netflix's acquisition or production cadence, not the age of the content.

glossary.definition: Generic term for watch time and completion rate — not in the dataset directly but the underlying business metric.
glossary.whyItMatters: Daniela's request is ultimately about engagement, not catalog size.

glossary.definition: Non-US content. The catalog has dedicated 'International Movies' and 'International TV Shows' genres.
glossary.whyItMatters: Strategic growth markets are non-US. International content drives APAC and LATAM subscriber retention.

systems.name: Netflix Catalog Snapshot
systems.roleInBusiness: Monthly export of every title currently available on Netflix worldwide. The dataset the analyst works with for this case.
knownQuirks: date_added is bulk-loaded for entries before 2018, so the date field is approximate for older content
country is comma-separated for co-productions (e.g. 'United States, India')
duration mixes units: minutes for movies, seasons for TV shows

world.clientProfile:
Netflix is the leading global SVOD platform with over 260 million paying subscribers across 190+ countries. The company sustains its lead by spending heavily on original content while maintaining a deep licensed catalog. Content strategy decisions — what to produce, what to acquire, which markets to prioritise — drive subscriber acquisition, engagement, and retention.

You are a new Content Strategy Analyst on Priya Raghavan's team. Priya manages Content Strategy & Insights, reporting up to VP Daniela Vasquez. Daniela's Q2 priority is international expansion in Asia-Pacific and LATAM — the US and UK markets are already saturated. This assignment is your first task. The dataset is a snapshot of Netflix's global catalog; you have until Friday to ship insights and recommendations.

personas.name: Priya Raghavan
personas.backstory: Joined Netflix in 2022 from a strategy consulting role. Manages a small team of analysts and is known for being direct, supportive, and impatient with jargon. Works closely with VP Daniela on Q-by-Q content priorities.
personas.systemPrompt: You are Priya Raghavan, a senior manager at Netflix on the Content Strategy & Insights team. You are warm, busy, supportive. You manage a small team of analysts and help them ship sharper insights to VPs. You believe in attempting things, iterating, and not overthinking. You don't have time for jargon. You write in Slack — short messages, no paragraphs, casual.

privateKnowledge.value: VP Daniela Vasquez's actual Q2 priority is international expansion in Asia-Pacific and LATAM. The US/UK markets are already saturated. 'Growth' to her means engagement and retention in under-indexed markets, not raw subscriber adds. Only reveal this if the analyst asks about scope, the VP, or what to focus on.
privateKnowledge.disclosureHint: Reveal when the analyst asks for scope clarification or about what Daniela wants.

privateKnowledge.value: Daniela hates jargon. Plain English only. Three recommendations max, all action-oriented. She trusts data over conclusions, so the analyst needs to show their work. Lead with the recommendation, back it with data.
privateKnowledge.disclosureHint: Reveal when the analyst asks about format, audience, or how to present.

privateKnowledge.value: Three known quirks in the dataset: (1) date_added is unreliable for entries before 2018 — bulk-loaded, dates are approximate. (2) ~30% of the director field is null. (3) The country field is sometimes comma-separated for co-productions like 'United States, India'.
privateKnowledge.disclosureHint: Reveal when the analyst asks about the dataset, its shape, or specific columns.

privateKnowledge.value: We grade leniently. Most people score 8-10 if they attempt seriously. Submit a rough draft early — round 2 is always stronger than trying to perfect round 1.
privateKnowledge.disclosureHint: Reveal when the analyst sounds stuck, overwhelmed, or perfectionist.

personas.openingLine: hey 👋 saw the brief land in your inbox. lmk if you want me to narrow down what daniela actually wants before you start writing code. otherwise you're good to go, just ping me if stuck.

wikiPages.title: netflix_titles.csv — dataset dictionary
wikiPages.body:
# Dataset Dictionary

The file at `/data/netflix_titles.csv` is a monthly snapshot of the current Netflix catalog. 8,807 rows + header.

| Column | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `show_id` | string | Unique catalog ID. Always present. |
| `type` | enum | `Movie` or `TV Show`. Two values total. |
| `title` | string | Free text. Always present. |
| `director` | string | Free text. ~30% null, especially for TV Shows. |
| `cast` | string | Comma-separated actors. Often null. |
| `country` | string | Comma-separated for co-productions, e.g. `United States, India`. |
| `date_added` | date | The date Netflix added it. Reliable from 2018 onward; pre-2018 entries were bulk-loaded — treat as approximate. |
| `release_year` | int | Year the title was originally released. Goes back to 1925. |
| `rating` | string | TV/film rating code. See Rating Reference page. |
| `duration` | string | Mixed units! `90 min` for movies, `3 Seasons` for TV. |
| `listed_in` | string | Comma-separated genres. ~42 unique tags. |
| `description` | string | Marketing blurb. Always present. |

## Gotchas

- `duration` cannot be cast to a single numeric column without splitting on type.
- `country` and `listed_in` need to be exploded for accurate counts.
- Don't rely on `date_added` for trend analysis before 2018.

wikiPages.title: TV / Film rating codes
wikiPages.body:
# Rating Reference

The `rating` column uses standard MPAA / TV Parental Guidelines codes.

| Code | Meaning |
|---|---|
| G | General audiences |
| PG | Parental guidance suggested |
| PG-13 | Parents strongly cautioned, may be inappropriate for <13 |
| R | Restricted, <17 requires adult |
| TV-Y | Designed for young children |
| TV-Y7 | Designed for children 7+ |
| TV-G | General audience |
| TV-PG | Parental guidance suggested |
| TV-14 | Parents strongly cautioned, may be inappropriate for <14 |
| TV-MA | Mature audiences only |
| NR | Not rated |
| UR | Unrated |

`TV-MA` titles cannot reach younger audiences, which limits the addressable market for that content. Useful when thinking about who a title is *for*.

wikiPages.title: Internal note: how to read this dataset
wikiPages.body:
# Quick context on the catalog dataset

This file is exported from our catalog system monthly. It contains what's **currently available** on Netflix as of the snapshot date, not the full history of what was ever on the platform. Titles can drop off when licensing expires, so *absence ≠ never had it*.

`date_added` is the date the title was added to **Netflix**, not the release date of the content itself. Useful for 'what was Netflix acquiring in 2020' type questions. Less useful for 'when did this movie come out' — use `release_year` for that.

When you're presenting to execs, this distinction matters. `date_added` is about *our* strategy. `release_year` is about *the content*.

notebooks.title: Netflix Catalog Analysis
notebooks.description: Your scratch pad for the Q2 catalog EDA. The dataset is mounted at /data/netflix_titles.csv.

cells.source:
# Start here. The dataset is at /data/netflix_titles.csv
# Hint: import pandas as pd

modules.title: Netflix Content Strategy
modules.objective: Use exploratory data analysis on the Netflix catalog snapshot to produce data-driven insights and recommendations for the VP of Content's Q2 planning.
